mcqs.question: What is the primary distinguishing characteristic of data used in batch processing?

options.text: It is always small in size.
It is bounded and finite.
It requires immediate processing.
It is generated by IoT sensors.

mcqs.explanation: Batch processing operates on a complete, finite dataset that has a defined beginning and end. This is in contrast to streaming data, which is a continuous, unbounded flow.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is a typical use case for stream processing rather than batch processing?

options.text: Generating monthly financial statements
Training a machine learning model on historical data
Detecting fraudulent credit card transactions in real-time
Performing ETL on last night's data warehouse load

mcqs.explanation: Fraud detection requires immediate analysis and action as each transaction occurs, which is the hallmark of stream processing. The other options involve processing large, accumulated datasets where latency is acceptable.

mcqs.question: In the Lambda Architecture, what is the role of the 'Speed Layer'?

options.text: To store the immutable master dataset
To pre-compute views from all historical data
To provide low-latency views by processing data in real-time
To serve merged results from batch and real-time views

mcqs.explanation: The Speed Layer is responsible for processing incoming data streams in real-time to provide low-latency, often approximate, results. This compensates for the high latency of the Batch Layer.

mcqs.question: Apache Spark Streaming processes data using which of the following models?

options.text: True event-at-a-time processing
Micro-batch processing
It does not have a specific model.
Bulk synchronous parallel processing

mcqs.explanation: Spark Streaming uses a micro-batch architecture. It buffers incoming data for very short intervals (e.g., 500ms) and processes these small batches using Spark's batch processing engine. This is different from frameworks like Flink which do true event-at-a-time processing.

mcqs.question: How does the Kappa Architecture fundamentally differ from the Lambda Architecture?

options.text: Kappa uses both batch and stream processing, while Lambda uses only stream processing.
Kappa uses only batch processing, while Lambda uses a hybrid approach.
Kappa uses only a single stream processing pipeline for all data, eliminating the dedicated batch layer.
Kappa architecture has higher latency than Lambda architecture.

mcqs.explanation: The Kappa Architecture simplifies the Lambda approach by using a single stream processing pipeline for all data. Historical reprocessing is handled by replaying data from the stream storage (e.g., Kafka), avoiding the need for a separate batch processing system and codebase.
